artifacts.content:
From: Michael Aaron Delgado <michael.a.delgado@gmail.com>
To: Emily Delgado <emily.r.delgado@gmail.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Sep 2024 21:47:03 -0700
Subject: Dentist + Dr. Morton appointments this fall

Hey Em,

Kids are finally down (Lucas’s dinosaur lineup on the pillow was a nice touch), so I took a few minutes to wrangle the health stuff we’ve been talking about.

1) Dentist: I called Harborview Family Dental in Proctor today on my lunch break. Earliest new-patient exam + cleaning they had was Thursday 12/12 at 8:00 a.m., which actually works well since I can head straight to the admin building afterward. I told them I’ve got that dull ache on the upper left when I chew almonds or crusty bread, the cold zings on a couple of lower teeth, and the old silver filling on the lower right that feels like sandpaper and keeps catching food. I also mentioned the jaw tightness in the mornings and that you hear me grinding at night.

I want to make sure they look closely around that inside left cheek area too. After the whole October 2023 scare at the Tacoma Oral Medicine Clinic with Dr. Raj Patel – the little white patch he biopsied – I’m still paranoid about oral cancer even though the results were benign. Every weird spot freaks me out now.

2) Dr. Morton: I’ve got a follow-up with Dr. Leah Morton at the Franciscan Medical Clinic on Pearl on 10/7 at 7:45 a.m. She wants to check in on the low-dose SSRI she started me on back in August before the EMR go‑live. Honestly, it helps me not grind my teeth quite so hard at night, or at least I’m not waking up with my jaw clamped like a vise as often.

Can you add both to the family calendar so we don’t double-book with conferences or soccer stuff for Sofia? If you need me home earlier any of those nights (so you don’t feel like you’re “married to Rainier Health Systems” again), flag it and I’ll block my calendar before Elena fills it with more EMR meetings.

Thanks for keeping us all semi-organized.

Love,
M

metadata.subject: Dentist + Dr. Morton appointments this fall

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Aaron Delgado <michael.a.delgado@gmail.com>
To: Elena Rodriguez <elena.rodriguez@rainierhealth.org>
Date: Mon, 3 Jun 2024 06:58:11 -0700
Subject: Scheduling around EMR Phase 2 + upcoming appointments

Hi Elena,

I wanted to give you a heads-up about a couple of medical/dental appointments over the next two months and how I’m planning to keep them from impacting EMR Phase 2.

After the confidential Performance Improvement Plan email from HR in May (“Performance Improvement Plan – EMR Phase 2 Deadlines”), I’m very aware I can’t afford any more surprises with time away from work. I take that warning seriously and I’m committed to staying on top of our interim milestones.

I’ve booked everything either first thing in the morning or late afternoon so I’m available during core hours and for anything critical. The main one to flag is a dental evaluation in December to deal with some sensitivity and bite issues. I’m trying to get a handle on the jaw clenching and nighttime grinding before our next big cutover weekend.

To be honest, I’m still a little shaken from the September 14, 2024 go-live on the main Tacoma campus, when I approved that temporary workaround that led to the low-severity medication-ordering glitch on the North Wing. Even though no patients were harmed and the incident report classified it as low severity, seeing my name as the responsible project manager in “Low-Severity Medication Order Delay – North Wing, 9/14/24” really drove home how important it is that I’m fully present during cutovers.

That’s why I’m keeping anything nonessential off my calendar for the next go-live window. If something urgent comes up, I can move the appointments; my priority is making sure we hit our EMR deliverables and avoid any repeat of that North Wing issue.

I’ve already blocked my calendar around the key build freeze and testing dates, but if you see any conflicts or want me on-site for specific checkpoints, let me know and I’ll adjust.

Best,
Michael

metadata.subject: Scheduling around EMR Phase 2 + upcoming appointments

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Aaron Delgado <michael.a.delgado@gmail.com>
To: Daniel Harper <dan.harper@gmail.com>
Date: Sun, 5 May 2024 22:14:27 -0700
Subject: Quick update on Dad, teeth, and life

Hey Dan,

Figured I’d send a longer note instead of another half-asleep text.

First, your sister is a champ. She’s been juggling grading, Sofia’s reading projects, Lucas’s dinosaur obsession, and my parents drama without biting my head off (too much).

You probably heard the short version, but in March/April Dad (Carlos) had that scare and ended up at St. Francis Hospital in Federal Way for a few days with complications from uncontrolled type 2 diabetes. Sitting in his room on the medical floor, watching him hooked up to monitors, was a wake-up call. I kept thinking, “I do not want to end up in the same boat with avoidable health stuff.” That’s part of why I’m finally getting serious about my teeth and the jaw grinding instead of just powering through.

On the lighter side, the kids loved when you were up last month. Sofia still talks about coding that little game with you. Lucas keeps asking when “Uncle Dan with the robots” is coming back.

Emily and I are also still unpacking that big argument we had back in March 2023 about my evening work hours. She told me she was tired of me being “married to Rainier Health Systems” and missing bedtime, which honestly stung because it was true. I’ve been trying to push appointments either super early or late afternoon so I’m not disappearing right when she needs backup with dinner and homework.

One more fun financial note: I’m *still* paying off the 2018 root canal and crown from Whitfield Family Dentistry — $63/month through June 2025. Combine that with the leftover bills from Sofia’s ER visit at Tacoma General in January 2022, and our “Delgado Family – Medical/Dental Payments” spreadsheet is a thing of beauty.

Anyway, that’s the state of the union. Come up again soon — we owe you tacos and a beer.

-M

metadata.subject: Quick update on Dad, teeth, and life

artifacts.content:
Title: Health Goals 2024 – teeth, stress, schedule
Date: 2023-11-18
Author: Michael Delgado

Rough brain dump while I’m thinking about it.

1. Teeth / mouth
- Follow through on getting a new dentist in Tacoma’s North End (closer than Whitfield Family Dentistry on Lakeview). Last exam was July 2023. Need someone who will talk me through things so I don’t flash back to that teenage filling where they started drilling before I was numb.
- Specific issues to bring up: upper left dull ache when chewing harder stuff (almonds, crusty bread), sharp cold sensitivity on a couple of lower teeth, gums looking red/puffy around back molars with occasional bleeding when I floss. Old silver filling on lower right feels rough and catches food.
- Mention the October 2023 biopsy at Tacoma Oral Medicine Clinic with Dr. Raj Patel – small white patch on inside left cheek. Biopsy came back benign, but I’m still scared of oral cancer.
- Ask about night guard. Jaw tight most mornings, especially around big deadlines. Emily says she can hear me grind.

2. Work / stress
- Since August 2024, I’ve been on that low-dose SSRI Dr. Leah Morton at Franciscan Medical Clinic prescribed when I told her about the jaw clenching and not sleeping before the EMR go‑live. It’s helping, but I should keep an eye on side effects.
- I wrote earlier in this “Health Goals 2024” doc that I feel self-conscious about how yellow my teeth look on Zoom, especially in meetings with senior leadership and the younger PMs who look so polished. Feels like it makes me look unprofessional and feeds the imposter syndrome.

3. Time / family
- Protect evenings for Sofia’s homework and Lucas’s bedtime so Emily doesn’t feel like I’m “married to Rainier Health Systems” again.
- Try to batch appointments early mornings so I’m not constantly asking for time off and stressing about work.

From: Michael Aaron Delgado <michael.a.delgado@gmail.com>
To: Harborview Family Dental <info@harborviewfamilydental.com>
Date: Mon, 18 Nov 2024 09:22:41 -0800
Subject: New patient – scheduling and treatment planning questions

Hi there,

My name is Michael Delgado. I’m scheduled for a new-patient exam and cleaning on Thursday, December 12 at 8:00 a.m. and had a few questions ahead of time.

I’ve had quite a bit of dental work over the years, including a root canal and crown on my upper right first molar (tooth #3) back in March 2018 with Dr. Karen Whitfield at Whitfield Family Dentistry on Lakeview Blvd. There were no major complications, but the tooth stayed very sensitive for a few weeks, and I’m a little anxious about more major work.

Right now I’m dealing with:
- Intermittent dull ache on the upper left side when chewing harder foods (nuts, crusty bread)
- Sharp sensitivity to cold on a couple of lower teeth
- Red, puffy gums with occasional bleeding when I floss around the back molars
- An old silver filling on a lower right molar that feels rough and catches food
- Jaw tightness in the mornings; my wife says she hears me grinding my teeth at night

I’m trying to be proactive about prevention rather than waiting for emergencies. Part of that is financial, honestly: after unexpected bills from that 2018 root canal/crown and my daughter Sofia’s emergency ER visit at Tacoma General in January 2022, I ended up on a payment plan. I still have a balance I’m paying down at $63/month through June 2025 (it’s literally a line item in our “Delgado Family – Medical/Dental Payments” spreadsheet).

Could you let me know:
1) Whether you offer payment plans for larger treatment plans?
2) If early-morning or late-afternoon appointments are possible for any follow-up work (I work full time in Healthcare IT at Rainier Health Systems and am trying not to miss key EMR deadlines)?

Thanks in advance for any info you can share. I’m looking forward to getting established with your office.

Best,
Michael Delgado
Tacoma, WA

metadata.subject: New patient – scheduling and treatment planning questions

artifacts.content:
Event: New Patient Exam & Cleaning – Harborview Family Dental
Date: Thursday, 2024-12-12
Time: 8:00–9:30 a.m.
Location: Harborview Family Dental, Proctor District, Tacoma
Attendees: Michael Delgado

Notes:
- Leave house (4178 Willow Bend Dr) by 7:25 a.m. to allow for traffic and parking.
- Light breakfast only so I don’t get lightheaded if they need to numb anything.
- Things to mention:
  • Upper left dull ache when chewing hard foods (almonds, crusty bread).
  • Cold sensitivity on lower teeth.
  • Red/puffy gums, small amounts of bleeding when flossing, especially around back molars.
  • Old silver filling on lower right that feels rough and traps food.
  • Jaw tightness in the mornings; Emily says she hears me grind.
  • History of many cavities + root canal and crown on upper right first molar (tooth #3) in March 2018 with Dr. Karen Whitfield (Whitfield Family Dentistry, Lakeview Blvd).
  • October 2023 biopsy at Tacoma Oral Medicine Clinic with Dr. Raj Patel – white patch on inside left cheek, benign but still anxious about oral cancer.
  • Since August 2024, on low-dose SSRI from Dr. Leah Morton at Franciscan Medical Clinic for stress/anxiety and jaw clenching.
  • Dad (Carlos) hospitalized at St. Francis Hospital in Federal Way spring 2024 for complications from uncontrolled type 2 diabetes – motivation to be proactive about my own health.
  • Still paying off 2018 root canal via $63/month plan through June 2025; also leftover bills from Sofia’s Jan 2022 ER visit at Tacoma General – ask about payment options.

- Block work calendar 7:30–10:00 a.m.; log in from home afterward. Do NOT schedule during any EMR go-live or testing milestones (remember North Wing medication-order delay incident 9/14/24 – need to be fully present for those).

metadata.title: New Patient Exam & Cleaning – Harborview Family Dental

artifacts.content:
Thread: Michael ↔ Mark
Date: 2023-10-27

Mark: You alive? Haven’t seen you on Thursday runs in weeks.

Michael: Barely 😂 Been a weird month.

Mark: Uh oh. Work or home?

Michael: Both, but this time mostly health anxiety.

Michael: I found this small white patch inside my left cheek in early Oct and totally spiraled.

Michael: Ended up at Tacoma Oral Medicine Clinic with a guy named Dr. Raj Patel. He did a biopsy right there.

Michael: Biopsy at Dr. Patel’s office came back ok, but now I’m paranoid about every weird spot in my mouth.

Mark: Damn, man. Glad it was benign. That would freak me out too.

Michael: Yeah. Between that and my teeth being a mess (sensitivity, old fillings, gums bleeding when I floss), I feel like I’m one coffee away from everything falling apart.

Mark: Speaking of coffee, you still mainlining French press every morning?

Michael: Of course. And then I complain my teeth are yellow on Zoom. I actually wrote in this “Health Goals 2024” doc that I feel like they make me look unprofessional next to the younger PMs. Imposter syndrome ftw.

Mark: Dude, no one is zooming in on your incisors during a meeting.

Michael: Tell that to my brain.

Michael: On top of that, sleep has been trash. Jaw’s tight every morning. Finally talked to my primary, Dr. Leah Morton at Franciscan Medical Clinic, and she put me on a low-dose SSRI to take the edge off the stress/anxiety.

Mark: How’s that going?

Michael: Honestly helps me not grind my teeth quite so hard at night, or at least I’m not waking up with my molars welded together.

Mark: When you’re ready, come back to Thursday runs. We can complain about getting old and compare dental bills.

Michael: Deal. Give me two weeks.

Hey Emily,

Just wanted to let you know about my dental checkup this morning and touch base on our plans for the weekend.

The dentist confirmed my teeth aren't sensitive to brushing or flossing, which is a relief, but I still experience some sensitivity when chewing on the upper left side. Thankfully, no sensitivity to sweets or hot foods, and no burning or swelling in my mouth either. I mentioned that I’ve never had any complications from extractions and that I don’t smoke, which they said are big positives for my dental health.

The dentist reminded me that I should keep up with brushing twice daily, sometimes a third time if I’ve had something sticky. I’m also trying to floss more consistently, aiming for 4–5 times per week, though I still miss a night now and then. They suggested I keep using the soft nylon toothbrush and alcohol-free fluoride mouthwash before bed, and to continue with the interdental brushes a few times a week.

They asked about my past orthodontic treatment, which I think has helped with my bite, even though I still find myself mouth breathing occasionally, especially during those stressful project deadlines. Interestingly, no unusual sounds or pain around my ear when eating, which is good news. Also, no unpleasant taste or bad breath, thankfully.

Now, about the weekend: I was thinking we could take the kids to Point Defiance Park and maybe the zoo. I know Sofia has been wanting to see the new tiger exhibit, and Lucas will enjoy the dinosaur-themed playground. Perhaps we could invite your brother Daniel over for dinner on Saturday if he’s free. I’ll call Mom and Dad to see if they want to join us on Sunday for a small get-together at our place; maybe Dad can help me with that loose cabinet door while Mom entertains the kids.

Let me know what you think!

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Dental Checkup and Family Weekend Plans
